title: March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
04 June 2021, 2:00pm
11 June 2021 : The last section of this paper, Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter , was first published on 4 June 2021 and has now been updated.
This paper follows COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters , June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 , June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated , September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 , and December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 , which describe the measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19, and how we responded to them. This paper also includes information about how we are managing the transition to a new design for the Quarterly Employment Survey (QES).
Information is presented in the following sections:
- Key events in the March 2021 quarter
- Data and methodology considerations
- Redesign of the Quarterly Employment Survey
- Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter

## Key events in the March 2021 quarter
Globally, the COVID-19 pandemic continued to affect trade and travel flows. New Zealand’s border remained closed to almost all travellers throughout the quarter, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel.
Alert level changes in the quarter:
- The discovery of several community cases of COVID-19 prompted Auckland to move into alert level 3 on 14 February 2021, with the rest of New Zealand moving to alert level 2.
- Auckland moved to alert level 2, and the rest of New Zealand back to alert level 1, on 17 February 2021. Auckland moved to alert level 1 on 22 February 2021.
- Further cases culminated in Auckland moving back to alert level 3 on 28 February 2021, and the rest of New Zealand to alert level 2. On 7 March 2021, Auckland moved to alert level 2 and the rest of New Zealand back to alert level 1.  Auckland moved back to alert level 1 on 12 March 2021.
Australia allowed New Zealand travellers from areas marked as ‘non-COVID designated hotspots’ to enter the country without mandatory quarantine from 16 October 2020.
Wage subsidy payments (called Wage Subsidy March 2021) were in place from 4 March 2021 to 21 March 2021, to assist employees and self-employed people affected by the alert level changes.
New Zealand’s COVID-19 vaccination programme commenced in February 2021 with border and managed isolation quarantine staff, high-risk frontline workers, people living in high-risk locations, and household contacts of these groups receiving the vaccine.

## Data and methodology considerations
The abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. While the March 2021 quarter will be less impacted than some of the previous quarters, the measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components are still affected, and we continue to use alternative data and methodologies where this is the case. Where conditions support it, we are progressively returning to standard data sources and methods.
We released information on measurement challenges and our approach to these in the following papers:
COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
June 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
We used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters to more accurately capture quarterly changes in activity. This was applied in the following cases:
- for components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to the alert level 3 and 4 lockdowns (for example employment-based indicators, which were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- for components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- for components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
The absence of further sharp shocks to economic activity means we are able to move toward resuming our usual quarterly methods for many components of GDP(P) and GDP(E). During the September and December 2020 quarters, we reverted some components back to their usual quarterly method. The same review process will be followed in the March 2021 quarter.
We will compare the level of activity implied by our alternative approach with the level of activity of our usual quarterly method, and resume use of our usual method once these are comparable. In practice this involves comparing the difference (in dollar terms) between the alternative indicator and the usual indicator for the current quarter, against the average quarterly variation in the usual indicator series. We will resume the usual indicator once these are comparable.
If they are not comparable, then we will use the alternative data source and continue to review the series in future quarters. We will have an opportunity to review and align the series once detailed data from the annual national accounts are available.
We will provide an update on the approach being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 11 June 2021, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter .

### Additive outlier review
As detailed in our previous papers (linked above), applying additive outliers (AOs) to unusual data points ensures that historical seasonal factors will not be adversely affected, and we can continue to produce credible seasonally adjusted results.
In Update on COVID-19 and seasonal adjustment , we signalled that we would be reviewing all AOs previously applied. We have concluded this review, and at this stage we will not be making any changes to AO treatments previously applied. We will conduct another review of our seasonal adjustment over COVID-19-affected quarters in 12 months’ time.
Incorporating post-shock data points that reflect a return to more normal levels of activity will enable us to have a better understanding of the underlying trends in the data. In addition, incorporating more data points into our review means that values strongly affected by COVID-19 will be less influential on our seasonal factors. This will enable us to make more informed decisions about the treatment of COVID-19-affected data points, which are less subject to large revisions in future.
We will continue our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points and will be testing all March 2021 quarter results to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to March, June, September and/or December 2020 quarter results will be retained in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter .

### Seasonal adjustment of travel services and the March 2021 quarter
New Zealand’s border has been closed to almost all travellers since 19 March 2020, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel. Quarantine-free travel between Australia and New Zealand has been in place since 19 April 2021, and the impacts of this will be reflected in Gross domestic product: June 2021 , scheduled for release on 16 September 2021.
Exports of travel services make a significant contribution to New Zealand’s economy. Prior to COVID-19 the peak travel season fell in summer. In the 2019 calendar year, March quarter exports of travel services were worth $5.5 billion, compared with around $4.0 billion in the December quarter, $3.5 billion in the June quarter, and $3.0 billion in the September quarter.
The restrictions on travel to New Zealand in place since March 2020 mean that the value of travel service exports have declined significantly to around $1.5 billion in each of the June, September, and December 2020 quarters.
The seasonal factors that we apply to GDP(E) account for the higher levels of exports of travel services usually observed in December and especially March quarters. Travel is a strongly seasonal component, and so the seasonal factors in December and March quarters rate activity down to reflect this.
In a multiplicative decomposition, the seasonal effects change proportionately with the trend. If the trend rises, so do the seasonal effects, while if the trend moves downward the seasonal effects diminish too. In an additive decomposition the seasonal effects remain broadly constant, no matter which direction the trend is moving in.
What this means is that when the size of a component falls to very low levels – as has occurred to exports of travel services – the seasonal component delivered by the algorithm shrinks proportionally .
However, at the aggregate GDP(E) level we observe a smaller magnitude of change to the level of activity, as GDP(E) encompasses a range of other components. Therefore, the seasonal component at this level of aggregation is different. If we take the December 2020 quarter as an example – while the level of travel services exports was low, total GDP(E) had returned to a level of activity similar to pre-COVID-19. As a result, the derived seasonal component remains similar in size to pre-COVID quarters.
This means that aggregate GDP(E) will include the contribution of travel service exports in the COVID-19-affected quarters, plus the effect of lost seasonal activity that we normally expect in a March quarter.
In GDP(P), travel related activity is distributed across a range of industries, and appears to have a softer seasonal contribution than what we observe in GDP(E).
We have maintained the use of multiplicative seasonal adjustment in the treatment of GDP(P), GDP(E), and their component series, as it is the most suitable approach for the underlying characteristics of these series.

### Some challenges contributing to differences between production and expenditure measures
Conceptually, our production and expenditure measures of GDP should align. Practically, the degree of coherence between the measures can be affected by differences in the data sources and methodologies that underpin the components of each measure. Timing, scope, and coverage considerations of the various data sources can affect the degree of coherence.
We noted in previous papers that the abrupt nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19 and efforts to contain it have resulted in a higher degree of uncertainty around our quarterly estimates for affected periods. This uncertainty is highest in the June and September 2020 quarters, which reflected the impacts of the nationwide alert level 4 lockdown, and the subsequent bounce back in activity once restrictions were lifted. In these quarters, we observed a lower degree of coherence between our production and expenditure measures. Such sharp changes in economic conditions can have varied effects on the data sources and assumptions underpinning our quarterly methodologies and amplify any pre-existing measurement challenges.
At this stage we continue to investigate four main areas where there may be potential to increase the overall coherence between GDP measures:
- measurement of inventories
- changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption
- expenditure on managed isolation quarantine and emergency housing.
If ongoing review of these areas yields new data or suggests further adjustment to methods, we may revise data for affected quarters.

#### Measurement of inventories
Inventories are a challenging item to measure and collect data on. The data used in GDP is a mix of data collected directly from respondents, and modelled data, which are released quarterly via the Business financial data (BFD). The modelling assumes relationships between sales and inventories (using annual data) to produce a quarterly estimate.
The global disruptions to activity caused by COVID-19 are likely to have had an effect on supply chains and the supply and demand of goods, and have had varied impacts on different types of businesses, depending on their industry, supply chains, and size. Our current data sources and methods, particularly regarding the modelled component of inventories, may not have been able to capture some of these COVID-19-related impacts.
We will be reviewing and monitoring our inventories data closely and may revise previous quarters, should additional data come to hand. Data released recently in Business operations survey: 2020 presents the proportion of businesses impacted by supply chain disruptions and the proportion of businesses who have reduced inventory levels, with industry and business-size breakdowns.  This is an example of data that may assist us in identifying COVID-19-related measurement issues in our current data sources.

#### Changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption
Most sub-components of GDP production use quarterly output indicators as a proxy for the change in value added. The underlying assumption of stable relationships between gross output, intermediate consumption, and value added can change following abrupt shifts in activity, such as those caused by the alert level 4 lockdown. Failing to account for changes in these relationships can lead to under- or over-statement of activity.
As detailed in previous papers, we assessed changes in gross output to intermediate consumption ratios in COVID-19-affected quarters and, where these were significant, applied a value-added indicator that was derived from sales and purchases data from the BFD.
While this approach has enabled us to account for large and identifiable changes in these relationships, it is possible that the cumulative impact of small, harder-to-identify changes may be contributing to lower accuracy at the aggregate level.
An example is the rapid change in business travel expenditure. The closure of borders to almost all travellers has affected the value of business travel, both inbound and outbound. Outbound business travel (business travel debits) is part of the intermediate consumption of businesses. While we have been able to account for changes in intermediate consumption to purchase ratios for some sub-industries, it is plausible that the decline in business travel debits could be having more widespread impacts.

#### Expenditure on managed isolation quarantine and emergency housing
The introduction of managed isolation quarantine (MIQ), combined with an increase in the provision of emergency housing services, has changed the proportion of accommodation services being consumed by government.
We have identified that MIQ was being captured in household consumption expenditure (HCE) instead of government final consumption expenditure (GFCE). Rectifying this will reduce HCE and increase GFCE in the September and December 2020 quarters, with total GDE relatively unchanged.
We have also identified that emergency housing was being captured in HCE (via the use of indicators from the Retail Trade Survey) and a small part in GFCE (which we source from the Crown’s Financial and Information System). We normally account for this quarterly mismeasurement when we incorporate updated annual benchmarks. However, the rapid changes in the provision of emergency housing services brought on by COVID-19 mean that the impact is more material.  Rectifying this will reduce HCE and increase GFCE in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters. Total GDE should be relatively unchanged.

## Redesign of the Quarterly Employment Survey
Data from the Quarterly Employment Survey (QES) is used as the quarterly indicator in a range of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. These are listed below in the Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter . Most QES-based components use total hours paid, with some components using QES employee counts as well. Depending on the component, the level or the movement of the QES is used. Some components use data on working proprietors from the QES, and a few use filled jobs or full-time employee counts.
The March 2021 release of labour market statistics included results from the redesigned QES. See Effects of the QES redesign on the March 2021 quarter statistics for further information. The move to the new sample design has involved changes to the sample and weights. While the changes have been back-cast to minimise inconsistencies in the timeseries, these series are provisional, and comparisons of March 2021 quarter data against the rest of the timeseries should be used with caution.
Employment-based indicators are generally weaker indicators of output and value added, as there is not always an immediate and direct link between changes in employment and changes in output. Also, employment indicators from the QES represent employment at a point in time during a quarter, rather than over a whole quarter. In COVID-19-affected quarters we have used other data sources, such as the BFD to measure changes in activity that our usual QES-based indicators were unable to capture. See our previous papers for further discussion on this.
The QES series used in compiling GDP(P) and GDP(E) are at a relatively detailed industry level. We have assessed the new QES series and decided that we will not use them in producing our March 2021 quarter estimates of GDP(P) and GDP(E). Due to the impact of COVID-19, most components that normally use QES indicators were instead using alternative data sources such as the BFD in the December 2020 quarter, and we plan to continue to use these alternative data sources in the March 2021 quarter. Series that used the QES in the December 2020 quarter will use alternative sources in the March 2021 quarter. We will reassess the new QES series when compiling our June 2021 quarter GDP estimates, and provide an update of our approach prior to that release.

## Summary of approaches for Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter
Please note that the information below is valid as at date of release and is subject to change. We will endeavour to provide updated information prior to the release of Gross domestic product: March 2021 quarter .
- Metal ore and non-metallic mineral mining and quarrying normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived value-added data from the BFD was used for the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will be again in the March 2021 quarter.
Output indicators are normally sourced from value of building work put in place and are directly collected for parts of private-sector heavy and civil construction.
Changes for the components of this industry:
- QES is normally used for the public-sector component of heavy and civil engineering construction, while the private-sector component uses the movement in the ‘Other construction’ category of gross fixed capital formation (GFKF). Derived value-added volumes from the BFD were used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and this method will be used again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Construction services is normally derived from the combined movement in residential and non-residential building construction, which is sourced from Value of building work put in place data. BFD data indicates issues in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios, and so derived BFD value-added data was used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter. We are currently investigating a permanent change of method.

#### Retail trade, accommodation, and food services
All sub-industries in this industry normally use output indicators sourced from Retail Trade Survey data. The following sub-industries used derived BFD value-added data (including change in stock levels) to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter:
- food and beverage services
- accommodation services.

#### Transport, postal, and warehousing services
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Air and space transport normally use passenger and freight revenue kilometres data, collected directly from major companies. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters we used derived BFD value-added volumes data to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption and will use this again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Scenic and sightseeing transport normally uses international visitor arrivals data. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, we used BFD sales-volumes data and will use this again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Other transport normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. Derived BDC value-added volumes were used to account for changes in the relationship between gross output and intermediate consumption in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will be again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Warehousing and storage services normally uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. We used derived BFD valued-added data for the June, September, and December 2020 quarters and will use this again in the March 2021 quarter.

#### Information, media, and telecommunications
Changes for the components of this industry:
- Publishing (except internet and music publishing) normally uses QES. BFD value-added data was used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and are likely to be used again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Motion picture and sound recording activities normally uses gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule, which overstated work done in previous quarters due to the impact of the wage subsidy. A composite indicator derived from the Household Labour Force Survey (HLFS) and employment indicators (EI) data was used in the previously June, September, and December 2020 quarters. We have decided to revert to the usual EMS indicator for the September, December 2020 quarters and the March 2021 quarter, following review of this data.

#### Professional, scientific, technical, administrative, and support services
These are the changes for the components of this industry:
- Professional, scientific, and technical services normally uses GST sales data. Deflated value-added data derived from the BFD was used for the June, September, and December 2020 quarters. We will revert to the usual method in the March 2021 quarter.
- Advertising, market research, and management services normally uses BFD sales-volumes data. We used deflated value-added data derived from the BFD in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters. We have reverted to the usual method in the March 2021 quarter.
- Travel agency and tour arrangement services normally uses QES indicators. In the March 2020 quarter, GST sales data was used to represent the change in activity. Deflated value-added data derived from the BFD was used for the June, September, and December 2020 quarters. As this component is still affected by the relative absence of international travel into and out of New Zealand, we will use BFD value-added data again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Employment and other administrative services normally use QES. In September and December 2020 quarters, we used BFD value-added data to account for changes in gross output and intermediate consumption ratios and will maintain this indicator in the March 2021 quarter.
- Building, cleaning, pest control, and other support services normally use QES indicators. Due to challenges in using the redesigned QES, we will use the BFD on data from the Employer Monthly Schedule (EMS) to represent activity.

#### Education and training
All education and training sub-industries normally use a method of interpolation of the annual without a quarterly indicator. The following approaches will be used to represent activity in this industry:
- Preschool education was estimated in the March 2020 quarter using an assumption of no activity for the days of the alert level 4 lockdown, and 100 percent activity at alert level 1. For the June and September 2020 quarter we used cumulative attendance hours. For the December 2020 quarter, we reverted to the usual method of interpolation of the annual with no indicator. In the March 2021 quarter, we will look at cumulative attendance hours again due to the alert level changes that occurred.
- Tertiary education was estimated using student visa data in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and is likely to be estimated that way again in the March 2021 quarter. We assume that remote learning undertaken during higher alert levels is equivalent to face-to-face learning.
- Adult, community, and other education was adjusted using unit-level information to estimate activity during alert level 4 in the March 2020 quarter. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, BFD sales was used and will be used again in the March 2021 quarter.

#### Healthcare and social assistance
The following sub-industries normally use QES indicators. See below changes to methods made in previous quarters. We will maintain the use of alternative indicators in the March 2021 quarter.
- The market component of hospital services used BFD sales volumes in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter.
- The market component of private medical and other health care services used BFD sales volumes in the March 2020 quarter. A composite indicator derived from low-level Household Labour Force Survey data and monthly employment indicator data was used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter.
- Residential care services and social assistance used BFD sales volumes in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter.

#### Arts, recreation, and other services
The following components of this industry used alternative indicators in previous quarters. We will maintain the use of alternative indicators in the March 2021 quarter.
- Heritage and artistic activities used BFD sales volumes in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters. We will use EMS data in the March 2021 quarter.
- Sport and recreation activities used BFD sales volumes in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters.
- Religious services normally uses QES. We will use a composite indicator derived from low-level Household Labour Force Survey data and monthly employment indicator data.
- Civil, professional, and other interest groups used derived value added from the BFD in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in the March 2021 quarter.
In cases where changes have been made to line up to corresponding components on the production side of GDP, the different underlying concepts between measurement of production and expenditure have been accounted for.

#### Household consumption expenditure
- For HCE on education, domestic expenditure was aligned with the approaches used for GDP(P) education sub-industries in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will be again in the March 2021 quarter. The measurement of education travel credits is consistent with the approach used in our balance of payments.
- Hospital services and out-patient services were adjusted to align with health and social assistance, using BFD sales-values data.
- Recreational services and cultural services both normally use QES indicators, in line with heritage and artistic activities and sport and recreation activities in the arts and recreation industry. These sub-industries used BFD sales values in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and will again in March 2021.
- Road passenger services and other passenger transport costs were both adjusted using electronic card transactions (ECT) data in March 2020 quarter. Low-level BFD sales-values data was used in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and this will be used again in the March 2021 quarter.
- We updated travel credit ratios to several categories in the June 2020 quarter and have maintained these ratios in the September and December 2020 and March 2021 quarters.

#### Gross fixed capital formation
The local government component of other construction uses interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the March 2020 quarter, an adjustment was made to account for the days in alert level 4 lockdown. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, sales data from the BFD was used.
Parts of the private and central government components of other construction use interpolation without a quarterly indicator. In the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, sales data from the BFD was used, and this will be used again in March 2021 quarter.
Land improvements usually use interpolation without a quarterly indicator. We used low-level movements from other construction to represent this component in the June, September, and December 2020 quarters, and this will be used again in the March 2021 quarter.
Tourist spending in New Zealand is normally estimated using data collected via the International Visitor Survey (IVS).
IVS data collection has ceased because of border closures introduced from March 2020. A new method for measuring international tourist expenditure has been developed to fill this gap. See Estimating visitor expenditure in New Zealand during the June 2020 quarter for further information about this new methodology.
For education export services, we estimate the tuition fees paid by overseas students from export education levy (EEL) data compiled by the Ministry of Education (MoE).
For the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters, we used updated 2019 EEL data from MoE and adjusted it using updated student visa numbers from Immigration New Zealand. See International trade: June 2020 quarter data sources and methods . The same approach will be used for the March 2021 quarter.
Ruvani Ratnayake 027 445 3174 info@stats.govt.nz

- Gross domestic product
